section.title: The Temptation in the Wilderness
section.sectionRationale: Immediately after the Father names Jesus His beloved Son and the Spirit descends, that same Spirit leads Him into the wilderness to be tested by the devil. Where Israel failed in the desert and the first Adam fell in the garden, the second Adam stands. Each temptation is answered not with power but with the written word of God, and the tempter withdraws until an opportune time.
section.thesis: The Spirit-anointed Son is tested at every point where Adam and Israel fell, and He conquers not by displaying His power but by trusting and obeying the word of His Father, proving Himself the faithful Son who will save His people.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the Spirit leads Jesus into the wilderness to be tempted; the temptation to turn stone to bread; the temptation to gain the kingdoms by worshiping the devil; and the temptation to test God at the temple, after which the devil departs until an opportune time.

units.label: Led by the Spirit into the wilderness
units.note: full of the Spirit, tempted forty days

verses.text: Then Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness,
where for forty days He was {{peirazo|tempted}} by the {{diabolos|devil}}. He ate nothing during those days, and when they had ended, He was hungry.

units.label: Bread alone
units.note: man shall not live on bread alone

verses.text: The devil said to Him, “If You are the Son of God, tell this stone to become bread.”
But Jesus answered, “It is {{gegraptai|written}}: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone.’”

units.label: All the kingdoms of the world
units.note: worship the Lord your God and serve Him only

verses.text: Then the devil led Him up to a high place and showed Him in an instant all the kingdoms of the world.
“I will give You authority over all these kingdoms and all their glory,” he said. “For it has been relinquished to me, and I can give it to anyone I wish.
So if You {{proskyneo|worship}} me, it will all be Yours.”
But Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only.’”

units.label: The pinnacle of the temple
units.note: do not put the Lord your God to the test

verses.text: Then the devil led Him to Jerusalem and set Him on the pinnacle of the temple. “If You are the Son of God,” he said, “throw Yourself down from here.
For it is written: ‘He will command His angels concerning You to guard You carefully,
and they will lift You up in their hands, so that You will not strike Your foot against a stone.’”
But Jesus answered, “It also says, ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’”
When the devil had finished every temptation, he left Him until an opportune time.

peirazo.gloss: To test, try, or tempt. The same word can mean a testing meant to strengthen or a temptation meant to destroy. The Spirit leads Jesus to be tested, yet the tester is the devil who means His ruin. Jesus is genuinely tempted, yet without sin, Hebrews 4:15.

diabolos.gloss: The devil, literally the slanderer or accuser. He is the personal enemy behind the temptations, claiming authority over the kingdoms of the world and seeking to divert the Son from the Father's path. He withdraws only until an opportune time.

gegraptai.gloss: It stands written, a perfect-tense form meaning what was written remains permanently in force. Three times Jesus answers with this word, each time citing Deuteronomy, wielding Scripture as settled, abiding authority against the tempter.

proskyneo.gloss: To bow down and worship, to render homage. The devil offers the kingdoms in exchange for a single act of proskyneo; Jesus refuses, insisting that worship belongs to the Lord God alone, and so keeps the path that leads through the cross to a kingdom rightly won.

basic.context: Right after His baptism, Jesus is led by the Spirit into the wilderness, where the devil tests Him over forty days. Hungry and alone, He faces three pointed temptations: to turn stone into bread, to gain all the kingdoms by worshiping the devil, and to force God's hand by leaping from the temple. Each time Jesus answers with Scripture. The devil finally leaves Him, but only for now.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative, but it is deeply patterned. Jesus relives the story of Israel, tested forty days as Israel was tested forty years, and He succeeds where they failed, answering each temptation from Deuteronomy, the book of Israel's wilderness lessons. He is also the second Adam, tested and faithful where the first Adam fell. Read it as both real history and rich fulfillment.
basic.originalLanguages: The word peirazo means both test and tempt: the Spirit leads Jesus to be tested, yet the diabolos, the slanderer, means His ruin. Three times Jesus answers with gegraptai, it stands written, citing Deuteronomy as settled authority. The devil offers the kingdoms for one act of proskyneo, worship, and Jesus refuses, giving worship to God alone.
basic.typology: Jesus is the true Israel, tested in the desert and proving faithful where the nation grumbled and fell. He is the second Adam, meeting the tempter's twist of God's word with trust and obedience rather than doubt. The forty days recall both Israel's forty years and Moses' forty days, gathering up the story of God's people in one obedient Son.
basic.christ: Jesus refuses to use His power for Himself, to take a shortcut to the kingdoms, or to test His Father's care. He wins not by might but by trusting and obeying the word. In this He is our substitute and our example: the sinless Son who is tempted in every way as we are, yet never sins, and who therefore can help us when we are tempted.
basic.god: God's word is the weapon that overcomes the tempter, showing that Scripture is enough. God the Father is worthy of exclusive worship and single-hearted trust. Even the testing serves God's purpose, proving the Son and preparing Him for His mission, though the tempter intends only harm.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 8:3, 6:13, and 6:16 are the three texts Jesus quotes. Genesis 3 shows the first Adam's fall by doubting God's word. Hebrews 4:15 says He was tempted in every way, yet without sin. James 4:7 tells us to resist the devil so he will flee.
basic.oneStory: Where Adam fell in a garden of plenty and Israel fell in a desert of want, the second Adam and true Israel stands firm. By trusting His Father's word rather than grasping for bread, power, or proof, Jesus begins to reverse the fall and to walk the obedient road that will lead to the cross, the throne, and the salvation of His people.

context.literary: The temptation forms a bridge between the baptism, where Jesus is declared Son, and the public ministry that follows. Luke frames it with the Spirit: full of the Spirit He enters, and in the Spirit's power He returns to Galilee. The three temptations are told in tight exchanges, each a challenge answered by a citation from Deuteronomy. Luke places the temple temptation last, ending in Jerusalem, the city toward which his whole Gospel moves.
context.historical: The Judean wilderness west of the Jordan is a harsh, barren place, fitting for a forty-day fast. The pinnacle of the temple was a high point of the temple complex overlooking a steep drop. To Jewish ears the forty days unmistakably recalled Israel's forty years and Moses' fasts, and the citations from Deuteronomy set Jesus consciously within Israel's wilderness story.
context.canonical: The scene answers Genesis 3: the second Adam faces the tempter and does not fall. It recapitulates Israel's testing and reverses its failures. Paul develops Jesus as the last Adam, Romans 5 and 1 Corinthians 15, and Hebrews presents Him as the sympathetic High Priest, tempted yet sinless, the ground of our confidence in every trial.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the Spirit-anointed Son tested at the outset of His ministry and proving faithful where Adam and Israel failed, to reveal that He overcomes by trusting and obeying God's word rather than by using power for Himself, and to prepare readers to see Him as both substitute and example in temptation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account describes a unique, once-for-all testing of the Messiah at the start of His mission. Yet it also models how believers meet temptation, by knowing and trusting Scripture and worshiping God alone. We are not the Messiah winning redemption, but we do follow His pattern of resisting the tempter with the word.

debates.question: In what sense could Jesus be genuinely tempted if He could not sin?

views.label: Real temptation, true sinlessness
views.case: Hebrews 4:15 says He was tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin. The temptations were real and felt, pressing on genuine human desires like hunger, yet His holy character meant He would not and did not yield.

views.label: The strength of the tested, not the weakness of the yielding
views.case: Some note that only the one who never gives in feels the full force of temptation; Jesus bore its whole weight without the relief of surrender, so His testing was more intense, not less, than ours.

debates.bearing: Either way, His temptation is real enough to sympathize with us and His sinlessness sure enough to save us; we lean on a Savior who understands and who never failed.

debates.question: Was the devil's claim to give the kingdoms of the world true?

views.label: A real but limited and borrowed authority
views.case: Scripture calls Satan the ruler of this world, John 12:31, so his offer has some reality; yet his authority is derivative and temporary, permitted by God, not ultimate.

views.label: A slander mixed with truth
views.case: As the slanderer, he exaggerates and lies; the kingdoms are the Father's to give to the Son, Psalm 2, so the offer is a counterfeit shortcut that bypasses the cross.

debates.bearing: Whatever authority the devil holds, Jesus refuses the shortcut; He will receive the kingdoms from His Father by the way of obedience and the cross, not by worshiping the enemy.

study.originalLanguages: The key word peirazo carries both senses at once: the Spirit leads Jesus to be tested, but the tester is the diabolos, the slanderer, who means His destruction. Jesus meets each assault with gegraptai, it stands written, a settled and abiding word, quoting Deuteronomy 8:3, 6:13, and 6:16, the very book of Israel's wilderness instruction. When the devil offers the kingdoms for a single act of proskyneo, homage or worship, Jesus answers that worship and service belong to the Lord God alone. He conquers not with power but with the word and with undivided worship, and the devil withdraws until an opportune time.

typology.type: Israel tested forty years in the wilderness
typology.fulfillment: Jesus tested forty days, succeeding where Israel grumbled and fell, and answering from Deuteronomy, Israel's wilderness book.
The second Adam meets the tempter's twist of God's word with trust and obedience, beginning to reverse the fall, Romans 5:18-19.

typology.type: Moses' forty-day fast and the giving of the word
typology.fulfillment: Jesus fasts forty days and lives by every word of God, embodying the obedience the law required.

study.christ: Jesus stands revealed as the faithful Son and true substitute. He will not turn His power to His own comfort, will not seize the kingdoms by a shortcut, and will not put His Father to the test. He overcomes by trusting the word and worshiping God alone, walking deliberately toward the cross rather than around it. Because He was tempted in every way, yet without sin, He is both the pattern for our resistance and the sympathetic High Priest who helps us, Hebrews 2:18 and 4:15. Where the first Adam listened to the serpent, the last Adam silences him with Scripture.
study.god: The Father who has just declared His delight in His Son now allows Him to be tested, and the testing proves the Son and readies Him for His mission. God's written word is shown to be sufficient, the sword that turns back every temptation. And God alone is worthy of worship: the whole contest turns on whether Jesus will give to the devil the homage that belongs only to the Lord. He will not, and in refusing He honors the Father and secures our salvation.

crossReferences.note: Man does not live by bread alone but by every word from God, the text Jesus quotes against the first temptation.
Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only, Jesus' answer to the offer of the kingdoms.
Do not test the Lord your God, Jesus' reply to the temptation at the temple.
We have a High Priest tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, the ground of our confidence in trial.

study.oneStory: In a barren wilderness the second Adam and true Israel stands where all before Him fell. Refusing to grasp for bread, power, or proof, Jesus trusts His Father's word and gives Him alone His worship. The tempter retreats, but only until an opportune time, and the shadow of the cross already lies across the story. The obedient Son has begun to undo the fall, walking the faithful road that will win the very kingdoms the devil could only counterfeit.

sermon.title: The Temptation of Jesus, Part One
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Luke, this message on Luke 4:1 shows how the devil aimed his temptations at both Christ's true humanity and His messianic mission, seeking to lure Him from the Father's will and the way of the cross, and how Christ would not be dissuaded. A faithful, expository treatment of this movement.

quiz.q: Who led Jesus into the wilderness, and why did He go there?

options.text: The Holy Spirit led Him there to be tested by the devil.
options.note: Verses 1 and 2 say Jesus, full of the Spirit, was led by the Spirit to be tempted by the devil.

options.text: He went to fast and pray in solitude for a season.
options.note: He did fast, but Luke's stated point is that the Spirit led Him there specifically to be tested.

options.text: He wandered there by chance after His baptism.
options.note: It was no accident; the Spirit purposefully led Him into the testing.

options.text: The devil dragged Him there against His will.
options.note: It was the Spirit, not the devil, who led Him, and Jesus went willingly.

quiz.q: How did Jesus answer each of the devil's temptations?

options.text: By quoting the written word of God, citing Deuteronomy each time.
options.note: Three times Jesus says it is written and answers from Deuteronomy 8 and 6.

options.text: By rebuking the devil with His own divine authority.
options.note: He is the divine Son, but Luke stresses that He answered with Scripture, not a bare display of authority.

options.text: By performing a miracle to prove He was the Son of God.
options.note: Refusing to prove Himself on demand was part of His victory; He worked no sign for the tempter.

options.text: By negotiating a compromise with the devil.
options.note: Jesus made no compromise; He refused every temptation outright.

quiz.q: What did the devil offer in exchange for worship?

options.text: Authority over all the kingdoms of the world and their glory.
options.note: In verses 6 and 7 he offers the kingdoms if Jesus will worship him.

options.text: Relief from His hunger after the long fast.
options.note: That was the pull of the first temptation, not the offer tied to worship in the second.

options.text: The protection of angels if He leapt from the temple.
options.note: That belongs to the third temptation, not the offer of the kingdoms.

options.text: Nothing; the devil demanded worship for its own sake.
options.note: He explicitly dangled the kingdoms and their glory as the price of worship.

quiz.q: How does Jesus' victory in the wilderness relate to Adam and Israel?

options.text: He stands firm as the second Adam and true Israel, succeeding where they fell.
options.note: Tested like Adam and like Israel in the desert, Jesus obeys where they disobeyed.

options.text: He simply gives us a good example of resisting temptation.
options.note: He is our example, but He is more, our victorious substitute who reverses the fall.

options.text: He shows that temptation is not really dangerous.
options.note: The temptations were real and severe; His victory was hard-won, not a sign that testing is trivial.

options.text: He proves that only extraordinary people can resist the devil.
options.note: The opposite: He wins with Scripture available to all, and equips His people to resist too.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the devil left Him 'until an opportune time'?

options.text: The conflict was not finally over; the tempter would return to oppose Jesus again.
options.note: Verse 13 signals that the struggle continues, reaching its height at the cross.

options.text: The devil was defeated in this round but not yet forever.
options.note: True and close, though the phrase specifically points ahead to renewed assaults, not merely a pause.

options.text: The devil gave up on Jesus entirely from then on.
options.note: The words say the opposite; he withdrew only for a time and would come back.

options.text: Jesus was now beyond any further temptation.
options.note: Luke deliberately notes that testing would come again at an opportune time.

questions: The first temptation came when Jesus was hungry and weak. When are you most vulnerable to temptation, and how might you prepare for those moments before they come?
Jesus met every temptation with 'It is written.' How well do you know the Scriptures you would need to answer the lies you most often hear?
The devil offered a shortcut to the kingdoms that bypassed the cross. Where are you tempted by a shortcut that avoids the harder, faithful road God is calling you to walk?
The whole contest turned on who would receive worship. What competes with God for your worship and single-hearted trust?
Jesus was tempted in every way, yet without sin, so He understands and can help you. How does it comfort you to bring your temptations to a Savior who has faced them and won?

translationNotes.note: Man shall not live on bread alone quotes Deuteronomy 8:3. The BSB footnote notes that some manuscripts add but on every word of God, filling out the sense of the Deuteronomy text; Luke's shorter reading still points to living by God's word.
Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only quotes Deuteronomy 6:13. Some later manuscripts add Get behind Me, Satan at the start of the verse; the BSB follows the earlier, shorter text.

The devil himself quotes Scripture, Psalm 91:11-12, about angels guarding God's servant. He cites it out of its trusting context to provoke a presumptuous test, showing that Scripture can be misused, and Jesus answers with Scripture rightly applied, Deuteronomy 6:16.

Luke's order of the temptations differs from Matthew's, ending at the temple in Jerusalem rather than on the mountain. The change fits Luke's Gospel-long movement toward Jerusalem, where the decisive testing and victory will come.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 4:1-13. Headings, line breaks, poem and note markers, and footnotes were skipped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage; none needed conversion.
